title: Common Issues - Physical
description: Practice identifying common physical network issues including cable opens and shorts, attenuation, electromagnetic interference (EMI), crosstalk, split pairs, incorrect pinouts, environmental factors, and physical layer troubleshooting techniques.
starterCode:
# COMMON ISSUES - PHYSICAL EXERCISE

## Part 1: Cable Faults

1. An _______ circuit occurs when a cable conductor is broken or disconnected, preventing signal transmission.
2. A _______ circuit occurs when two conductors make unintended contact, creating an alternate current path.
3. A cable _______ is a wiring error where conductors are connected to the wrong pins at one or both ends.
4. A _______ pair fault occurs when wires from different twisted pairs are incorrectly combined on terminal pins.
5. Split pairs pass a basic continuity test but fail _______ testing because the twist ratio is disrupted.
6. A _______ cable has the transmit and receive pairs reversed, historically used for connecting similar devices.
7. An incorrect _______ standard (T568A vs T568B) at one end creates a crossover instead of a straight-through cable.

## Part 2: Signal Degradation

8. _______ is the loss of signal strength as it travels along a cable, measured in decibels (dB).
9. Attenuation increases with cable _______, which is why maximum cable lengths are specified for each standard.
10. Ethernet over twisted-pair cables has a maximum distance of _______ meters (328 feet) per segment.
11. _______ loss occurs when light escapes from a fiber optic cable due to excessive bending.
12. A _______ bend in fiber optic cable exceeds the minimum bend radius, causing permanent signal loss.
13. _______ dispersion in fiber causes different wavelengths of light to travel at different speeds, distorting signals.
14. Signal _______ is the distortion of a signal waveform as it propagates through a medium.

## Part 3: Electromagnetic Interference

15. EMI stands for _______ Interference, caused by external electromagnetic fields affecting cable signals.
16. Common EMI sources include electric _______, fluorescent lights, and heavy machinery.
17. _______ cables use twisted pairs to reduce EMI; the twisting cancels out electromagnetic interference.
18. _______ (Shielded Twisted Pair) cables provide additional EMI protection with metallic shielding.
19. _______ cables are immune to EMI because they transmit light signals rather than electrical signals.
20. RFI stands for Radio _______ Interference, a subset of EMI in the radio frequency range.
21. Proper cable _______ away from EMI sources is essential to minimize interference.

## Part 4: Crosstalk Issues

22. _______ is the unwanted transfer of signals between adjacent wire pairs or cables.
23. NEXT stands for _______ Crosstalk, measured at the end where the signal originates.
24. FEXT stands for _______ Crosstalk, measured at the opposite end from the signal source.
25. _______ crosstalk occurs between pairs within the same cable.
26. _______ crosstalk (alien crosstalk) occurs between pairs in adjacent cables.
27. Higher category cables (Cat6a vs Cat6) have improved _______ performance to reduce crosstalk.
28. Cable _______ (twists per inch) varies between pairs to minimize crosstalk between them.

## Part 5: Environmental and Physical Factors

29. Extreme _______ (too hot or too cold) can degrade cable performance and shorten cable lifespan.
30. _______ exposure can damage cable jackets and cause insulation breakdown over time.
31. Cable _______ radius specifications must be followed to prevent internal conductor damage.
32. Exceeding the maximum cable _______ force can stretch conductors and damage internal structure.
33. _______ damage from rodents or insects can compromise cable insulation and conductors.
34. Water _______ into cable pathways can cause shorts, corrosion, and signal degradation.
35. Improperly terminated _______ (RJ45 plugs) can cause intermittent connectivity and signal loss.
36. A _______ bounce occurs when a port repeatedly goes up and down due to a faulty cable or connector.
37. Dirty or damaged fiber optic _______ can cause significant signal loss and connection failures.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Cable Faults

1. An **open** circuit occurs when a cable conductor is broken or disconnected, preventing signal transmission.
2. A **short** circuit occurs when two conductors make unintended contact, creating an alternate current path.
3. A cable **miswire** is a wiring error where conductors are connected to the wrong pins at one or both ends.
4. A **split** pair fault occurs when wires from different twisted pairs are incorrectly combined on terminal pins.
5. Split pairs pass a basic continuity test but fail **crosstalk** testing because the twist ratio is disrupted.
6. A **crossover** cable has the transmit and receive pairs reversed, historically used for connecting similar devices.
7. An incorrect **pinout** standard (T568A vs T568B) at one end creates a crossover instead of a straight-through cable.

## Part 2: Signal Degradation

8. **Attenuation** is the loss of signal strength as it travels along a cable, measured in decibels (dB).
9. Attenuation increases with cable **length**, which is why maximum cable lengths are specified for each standard.
10. Ethernet over twisted-pair cables has a maximum distance of **100** meters (328 feet) per segment.
11. **Bend** loss occurs when light escapes from a fiber optic cable due to excessive bending.
12. A **macro** bend in fiber optic cable exceeds the minimum bend radius, causing permanent signal loss.
13. **Chromatic** dispersion in fiber causes different wavelengths of light to travel at different speeds, distorting signals.
14. Signal **degradation** is the distortion of a signal waveform as it propagates through a medium.

## Part 3: Electromagnetic Interference

15. EMI stands for **Electromagnetic** Interference, caused by external electromagnetic fields affecting cable signals.
16. Common EMI sources include electric **motors**, fluorescent lights, and heavy machinery.
17. **UTP** cables use twisted pairs to reduce EMI; the twisting cancels out electromagnetic interference.
18. **STP** (Shielded Twisted Pair) cables provide additional EMI protection with metallic shielding.
19. **Fiber** cables are immune to EMI because they transmit light signals rather than electrical signals.
20. RFI stands for Radio **Frequency** Interference, a subset of EMI in the radio frequency range.
21. Proper cable **routing** away from EMI sources is essential to minimize interference.

## Part 4: Crosstalk Issues

22. **Crosstalk** is the unwanted transfer of signals between adjacent wire pairs or cables.
23. NEXT stands for **Near-End** Crosstalk, measured at the end where the signal originates.
24. FEXT stands for **Far-End** Crosstalk, measured at the opposite end from the signal source.
25. **Internal** crosstalk occurs between pairs within the same cable.
26. **External** crosstalk (alien crosstalk) occurs between pairs in adjacent cables.
27. Higher category cables (Cat6a vs Cat6) have improved **shielding** performance to reduce crosstalk.
28. Cable **twist** (twists per inch) varies between pairs to minimize crosstalk between them.

## Part 5: Environmental and Physical Factors

29. Extreme **temperature** (too hot or too cold) can degrade cable performance and shorten cable lifespan.
30. **Moisture** exposure can damage cable jackets and cause insulation breakdown over time.
31. Cable **bend** radius specifications must be followed to prevent internal conductor damage.
32. Exceeding the maximum cable **pull** force can stretch conductors and damage internal structure.
33. **Pest** damage from rodents or insects can compromise cable insulation and conductors.
34. Water **ingress** into cable pathways can cause shorts, corrosion, and signal degradation.
35. Improperly terminated **connectors** (RJ45 plugs) can cause intermittent connectivity and signal loss.
36. A **link** bounce occurs when a port repeatedly goes up and down due to a faulty cable or connector.
37. Dirty or damaged fiber optic **connectors** can cause significant signal loss and connection failures.
hints: Open circuits are broken conductors; short circuits are unintended contact between conductors causing alternate paths.
Split pairs pass continuity tests but fail crosstalk tests because the twist pairing is disrupted.
Attenuation is signal loss over distance; twisted-pair Ethernet maximum is 100 meters per segment.
Macro bends exceed fiber minimum bend radius; chromatic dispersion affects different wavelengths differently.
EMI = Electromagnetic Interference; common sources include motors, fluorescent lights, and heavy machinery.
STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) adds metallic shielding; fiber optic cables are completely immune to EMI.
NEXT = Near-End Crosstalk; FEXT = Far-End Crosstalk; alien crosstalk occurs between adjacent cables.
Cable twist rate varies between pairs to minimize crosstalk; higher category cables have better performance.
Environmental factors include temperature extremes, moisture ingress, UV exposure, and pest damage.
Improperly terminated connectors and dirty fiber connectors are common causes of intermittent connectivity.
